prompt: Why do coffee shops so often have similar aesthetics (exposed brick, pendant lights, light wood) across very different cities? Offer a serious analysis, not a joke.
response:
The converging look of cafés in Seoul, Brooklyn, Copenhagen, Mexico City and Melbourne is not a conspiracy, nor is it merely lazy copying. It is the predictable visual by-product of several mutually reinforcing forces that have become global since the late 1990s:

1. A single, transnational design code for “third-wave” coffee  
   Third-wave coffee treats the drink as an artisanal craft analogous to wine. The movement’s pioneers—mostly North-American and Antipodean—needed a visual language that signaled “transparent, authentic, non-industrial.” They landed on a vernacular that could be built cheaply in old retail shells:  
   - Expose whatever structural honesty already exists (brick, concrete, steel joints).  
   - Use filament bulbs and factory-cage pendants because they reference the Modern workshop and cost little at salvage yards.  
   - Specify pale maple or birch counters that show stains immediately—proof that the barista wipes surfaces obsessively.  
   That code was written into the first brand manuals, then carried abroad by consultants, franchisees and Instagram mood-boards.

2. Global supply chains for hospitality fit-outs  
   Alibaba, Wayfair and a handful of Scandinavian contract-furniture firms now sell the same Bentwood chair, Marset pendant and hexagonal café table everywhere. A roaster in Santiago can order the “Brooklyn” package online and receive it in six weeks; the price is lower than commissioning local craftsmen. Once the furnishings arrive, the room ends up looking like the catalog photo, which itself looked like cafés in Brooklyn.

3. Commercial real-estate filtering  
   Coffee shops colonize fringe districts where rents are low but foot traffic is rising. These districts are full of century-old industrial or retail shells whose cheapest renovation strategy is “strip back instead of build up.” Exposed brick is therefore not an aesthetic choice first; it is the line item the landlord and tenant can both agree to leave untouched. Over time landlords learn that this rawness is a rent-premium feature and begin marketing spaces that already look like Brooklyn lofts even in Bangkok.

4. Social-media visual network effects  
   Instagram rewards instantly recognizable scenes. Pale wood reflects light, brick provides texture, and Edison bulbs give warmth at 3:00 p.m.—the hour most likely to be posted. After thousands of iterations, the algorithm has taught owners which combination harvests the most geotags and, by extension, walk-ins. The feedback loop is now faster than any local culture can resist: within six months a new café can iterate toward the optimum “like” yield regardless of continent.

5. Cultural cosmopolitanism as product positioning  
   For the emerging middle class in many cities, drinking specialty coffee is a badge of global orientation. A space that looks “Portland” signals that the beans are traceable, the milk is oat, the payment terminal accepts Apple Pay. The more familiar the setting feels to someone who has traveled (or merely swiped), the lower the cognitive barrier to paying five dollars for a beverage. Ironically, local motifs are riskier: they may read as “ethnic” or “traditional,” undermining the cosmopolitan cue that the roaster wants to send.

6. Regulatory convergence on accessible, low-flame interiors  
   Pendant lights and naked bulbs avoid the dropped ceilings that once hid asbestos and sprinklers. Light-toned woods are usually FSC-certified, slip-resistant, and class-B fire rated—the same SKU satisfies code from Berlin to Busan. Once again, the cheapest compliant product is the globally traded one, so the room ends up looking the same.

7. Labor economics and operational layout  
   The espresso bar is now a globally standardized workstation: Mazzer grinders, La Marzocco linea, under-counter Mythos. The optimal flow—from till to grinder to machine to pick-up—fits neatly into a 3 × 8 m footprint. Because that footprint is identical in every city, the surrounding stools, counters and pendant“task lighting” drop into place like Lego.

What is not driving the look is any deep cultural preference for brick or birch. It is the cumulative outcome of efficiency pressures—design, logistics, finance, algorithmic attention and real-estate arbitrage—filtered through a single, rapidly circulating visual code. Once the returns to that code diminish (they already are in a handful of avant-garde cafés experimenting with maximalism and color), a new default will emerge and, within a decade, will be just as ubiquitous.
